flashcards.Back: The type of connection used by a lightweight access point to the wired network. it typically uses an access mode port to terminate the CAPWAP tunnel.
The type of connection often used by standalone access points, requiring an 802.1Q trunk link to support multiple SSIDs mapped to different VLANs.
The primary physical interface on a Wireless LAN Controller used for user data and management traffic. it must connect to a switch port in 802.1Q trunk mode.
A dedicated physical port on a WLC used for out-of-band management and system recovery. it must connect to an access mode port on a management VLAN.
A physical serial port (RJ-45 or USB) used for local, asynchronous out-of-band access to the WLC command-line interface.
A dedicated physical port used to interconnect two controllers in a High Availability (HA) pair for stateful synchronization and failover.
A configuration on a Cisco WLC that bundles all physical distribution ports into a single logical link, providing high-speed redundant connectivity.
The switch configuration required when connecting to a WLC with LAG enabled. it must be configured as an 'on' (unconditional) bundle as WLCs do not support LACP/PAgP.
A logical interface used for in-band management traffic, CAPWAP control tunnels, and communication with other infrastructure like RADIUS servers.
A logical interface created by an administrator to map a specific WLAN (SSID) to a corresponding wired VLAN ID.
A legacy logical interface (in older WLC versions) dedicated to termination of CAPWAP tunnels from access points. its functions are now combined into the management interface.
A logical interface used to support mobility management, DHCP relay, and the web-based login page for guest users.
The standard method for delivering DC electrical power to access points over standard UTP cabling, eliminating the need for local power outlets.
An enhanced PoE standard providing up to 30W of power per port, necessary for high-performance 802.11ac and 802.11ax access points.
Universal Power over Ethernet. A Cisco-proprietary standard providing up to 60W or 90W of power per port for demanding network devices.
Administrative access to a WLC through the same logical network path used for wireless data and AP control traffic.
Administrative access to a device through a dedicated physical network path (like the Service Port) that is isolated from standard data traffic.
The process where an AP wraps user Ethernet frames inside UDP packets to transport them across the Layer 3 wired network to the WLC.
The discovery mechanism where an AP finds a WLC on a different subnet using DHCP Option 43, DNS lookups, or a pre-configured list of controller IPs.
The foundational wired backbone network that provides connectivity between access points and the rest of the organization's resources.
A high-availability feature where a standby WLC maintains exact copies of client sessions from the active WLC, allowing for zero-downtime failover.
A cluster of Wireless LAN Controllers that share information about client stations, enabling seamless roaming and security policy enforcement across a large campus.
The physical point where the service provider network ends and the customer's wireless infrastructure begins, usually at a WAN edge router or switch.
Software (like PuTTY or Tera Term) used on a computer to connect to the WLC console port for initial setup or troubleshooting.
The standard communication rate for Cisco console ports, which is 9600 bits per second (baud).
The use of multiple hot-swappable power modules in a controller to prevent a single hardware failure from taking down the entire wireless network.
A software service running on the controller that can automatically provide IP addresses to wireless clients if a central DHCP server is not available.
A switch port assigned to a single VLAN that connects to a lightweight AP. The AP's management IP resides in this VLAN broadcast domain.
A switch port configured with 802.1Q tagging that connects to a WLC distribution port, carrying traffic for multiple wireless-mapped VLANs.
The action of the WLC adding an 802.1Q header to user data frames as they leave the controller and enter the wired distribution switch infrastructure.
